Row.Text: Citizens emigrate from unhappy, struggling cities to happier, more prosperous ones, both within and between civilizations, driven by a Prosperity model (food, production, happiness, war, siege, starvation). Migration counts are reported as historically representative populations, aligned with the Demographics mod's scaling.
How emigration reports the number of people who move. "Both" shows the Civ population number and the historically-scaled people count (matching the Demographics mod); the others show only one.
Open the full set of Emigration tuning knobs in a separate window. These are starting points, not playtested-balanced values; the intensity preset above sets sensible defaults for most of them.
Overall emigration intensity. Low = rare moves; High = frequent. "Custom" keeps the Advanced values below. Applying a preset updates the Advanced values, reopen Options to see them refresh.
Pressure a settlement must build before one population point emigrates. Lower = more frequent emigration.
Turns a settlement rests before it can lose another population point.
Cap on how many population points move across the whole world each pass.
Run the emigration pass once every N of your turns. Raise it to ease per-turn cost on large saves, and, if the Demographics mod is installed, lower its sample frequency first.
If off, people only move between cities of the same civilization.
Whether minor / city-state settlements take part in migration.
Only consider foreign cities your civilization has met.

Apply small, hand-tuned per-leader and per-civilization adjustments (magnet damping, integration speed, war-time retention, source bias) so outliers behave distinctly. Off = every civ uses the same global model.

How far the per-leader/civ adjustments are allowed to diverge from the global model. 1.0 = full distinct identities; lower values flatten the differences between civilizations toward the same model to curb snowballing; 0 = fully flat (same as turning the table off).
Enable the Open / Closed Borders policies' effect on immigration (the policy cards are added in the policy menu and cost or grant Influence). Off = the policies have no migration effect.
How much immigration a Closed Borders policy lets through (0.4 = about 60% turned away). Lower = more closed. Only used when border policies are on.

How strongly a Closed Borders policy keeps your own people from leaving for rival civs (0.6 = their pull toward other civs cut to 60%; 1 = off). Lower = stronger retention. Only used when border policies are on.
How much an Open Borders policy increases immigration (1.5 = +50%). Higher = more open. Only used when border policies are on.
How much per-capita food pulls migrants toward a city.
How much per-capita production (the "work available" proxy) pulls migrants.
How strongly a city's net happiness attracts migrants.
How much a city's size counts against it (so small thriving towns still attract).

Replace the flat happiness weight with a nuanced model: happiness is measured relative to the world average, its pull saturates (so a very happy city does not become a dominant magnet) while misery still strongly repels, and happiness amplifies a city's economy instead of dwarfing it. Off = the legacy linear weight.
How many points above the world-average happiness count as "very happy" before the pull saturates. Higher = happiness keeps mattering longer. Only used by the shaped model.
How much a happy city's economy is amplified (and a miserable one suppressed) as a migration draw. Only used by the shaped model.
Credit back some of the unhappiness a city suffers purely from urban overcrowding, so a deliberately tall/dense city isn't double-punished (its density already suppresses its yields). 0 = off.
Urban population a city can hold before overcrowding counts against it (mirrors the game's overcrowding threshold). Only used when the discount is on.
Score penalty per point of accumulated combat intensity in a city's borders.
Ceiling on the violence penalty, so a rout saturates rather than runs away.
How fast accumulated violence fades with no new fighting (lower = fades faster).
War: violence needed to flee an invader
Minimum violence before refugees flee directionally away from the nearest enemy.
War: weight of fresh city damage
Intensity added when a city takes new district damage in a turn (an assault spike).
Intensity added each turn while a city center stays damaged.
War: weight per pillaged tile (0 = off)
Intensity per pillaged improvement in a city's borders, per turn. 0 skips the scan.
War: use the siege model (capped + escalating)

Make war-driven emigration escalate the longer a city is besieged, but cap the total a city can lose to war so it can't be fully depopulated without a capture (the remnant digs in). Off = the legacy flat violence penalty.
How many turns a siege must last to reach its full emigration pressure (a fresh raid starts gentle and ramps up). Only used by the siege model.
War (siege): max share lost to war
The most of a city's population (as a fraction of its size when the siege began) that war-driven emigration can take before it stops. Only used by the siege model.
War: refugee surge per turn (1 = off)

The most rural population a heavily besieged city can shed in a single turn. War refugees then leave in bursts that scale with the intensity of the fighting, instead of a steady one-per-turn trickle, still bounded by the siege loss cap. 1 = off (the old trickle).

How strongly refugees fleeing a war avoid the civilization that attacked them, preferring their own cities first, then any third party, and the aggressor last. 0 = off (refugees ignore who started the war).
How strongly war refugees prefer to resettle within their own civilization before crossing to another. Only used when "avoid the aggressor" is on.
Pull penalty per hex between source and destination. Higher = more local migration.
How strongly refugees prefer destinations away from the nearest invader.
Extra reluctance to migrate across a civilization border.

How strongly a civilization in acute crisis (war/disaster) pushes its refugees to flee ACROSS BORDERS to another empire, rather than relocating to its own (equally-stricken) cities and dying there. Higher = more cross-civ escape. 0 = off.
How much a base-game Open Borders agreement between two civilizations eases migration between them, both directions. 0 = off.

The longest a migrant can spend traveling before arriving. They leave the source the turn they depart; the actual delay scales with the distance to their destination (farther = longer), up to this cap. War refugees always take at least a turn. If their destination is destroyed while they are en route, they perish in transit. 0 = instant arrival.

Assimilation "load" added to the receiving civilization for each migrant it absorbs. The civ pays a per-turn cost proportional to its current load, which decays over the assimilation duration. 0 = off.
Multiplies the load added by (1 + this × destination population), so absorbing into a large settlement adds more, the brake on runaway population magnets.
How fast the assimilation load fades each turn, this sets the duration of the cost. Lower = shorter (migrants integrate faster); higher = the cost lingers longer.
Assimilation: happiness per turn per load
Happiness drained each turn per unit of assimilation load. 0 = off. (Inferred lever, if it no-ops in your build, use the gold cost.)
Assimilation: gold per turn per load
Gold drained each turn per unit of assimilation load. 0 = off. (Probe-confirmed deduction lever, including across civilizations.)
Cost: happiness per held migrant unit

Happiness drained each turn per UNIT_MIGRANT a civilization is holding (unsettled), scaling with the count, so overflow migrants must be settled, not hoarded. 0 = off. (Inferred lever, see the gold cost note.)
Cost: gold per held migrant unit
Gold drained each turn per UNIT_MIGRANT a civilization is holding, scaling with the count. 0 = off. (Probe-confirmed deduction lever.)

Make a civilization that's absorbing lots of migrants a less attractive destination for further migration (it's busy integrating newcomers), scaling with how much it's digesting. A structural brake on runaway magnets. 0 = off.

Make a civilization whose population has pulled far ahead of the field a less attractive destination for cross-civ migration: a self-correcting brake on a runaway leader that grows with how far ahead it is (0 off / 8 gentle / 15 standard / 28 strong). It never impedes a leader's OUTflow or internal moves. 0 = off.

How far a civilization's population may run ahead of the world-average civ before the anti-snowball headwind starts to bite, as a multiple of fair share. Lower = the brake engages sooner (more egalitarian); higher = only an extreme runaway leader is slowed.
Let environmental disasters (floods, volcanoes, plagues, …) push population out of struck cities as climate/disaster refugees, alongside war and unhappiness. Off = disasters don't affect migration.
How strongly accumulated disaster distress in a city pushes its people to leave. Only used when disasters drive migration.
How fast a disaster's distress fades once it passes (lower = fades faster). Only used when disasters drive migration.
Let refugees fleeing an infected city seed a smaller outbreak-distress at their destination, migration as a contagion vector, beyond the game's trade-network spread.
How chatty migration notifications are: 0 = off, 1 = important only (refugee crises and severe disasters, the default), 2 = verbose (a summary every turn there's notable migration).
Only notify for disasters at or above this severity (0 = even minor events, 3 = only legendary ones like a great eruption). Minor disasters still affect migration silently.

Which disasters pop up on screen (the notifications log always keeps every severe one regardless): 0 = off, log only; 1 = only disasters that strike a settlement, so they actually drive migration (the default); 2 = any disaster at or above the minimum severity. Combine with "min disaster severity" to tune how bad is bad enough.
The fewest turns between important notifications, so they can't bunch up. 0 = no limit.
How many people (cumulative) a civilization must displace before a refugee-crisis headline fires, once per multiple crossed, so a long war makes a few headlines, not one per turn.
Show an on-demand panel explaining why a selected settlement is gaining or losing population: the dominant cause, where its people are pulled, the assimilation cost, and what you can do about it.
Outlet: attrition when there's no refuge

Keep migration from being a closed system: a trapped, badly distressed city (siege, starvation, heavy violence or disaster) with nowhere viable to flee loses population, people die or scatter rather than stay forever.

How dire a city's situation (siege/starvation/violence/disaster, as a percentage penalty) must be before trapped population starts to die off. Higher = only the most desperate cities. Only used when the outlet is on.
Pressure a trapped, distressed city must build before it loses one population point. Lower = faster die-off. Only used when the outlet is on.
(people arriving in this civilization from elsewhere)
(people displaced by war, disaster, or conquest)
Population displaced by war, disaster, and conquest.
{1_People} were taken from {2_City} when it changed hands.
Switch between the Civ's own population numbers and scaled people
Playback timeline appears after a couple of turns, once there's population history to scrub through.
Raise this city's happiness, or slot an Anti-Immigration Stance to retain them.
A neighbor is out-prospering this city; grow its yields to keep people home.
Refugees flee the fighting; relieve the siege or make peace to stem the outflow.
Disaster displacement - it subsides on its own as the distress decays.
Trapped with nowhere to go - open a route out or relieve the distress.
It continues until you address the cause.
Those people are gone for good.
{1_Dest} pays about {2_Gold} gold/turn to assimilate them.

Welcome [icon:YIELD_POPULATION] migrants from around the world. Immigration to your cities rises by 50%, and your cosmopolitan reputation earns [icon:YIELD_DIPLOMACY] Influence each turn: +1, +2, then +3 across the ages.

Close your borders to the world. Foreign [icon:YIELD_POPULATION] newcomers fall to 40% (about 60% fewer), and far fewer of your own citizens leave for rival civilizations. A mobilized home economy adds [icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production in every city: +2, +3, then +4 across the ages. Isolation has its price in [icon:YIELD_DIPLOMACY] Influence each turn: -2, -3, then -4 across the ages.

[icon:YIELD_POPULATION] Newcomers strengthen your research. Gain +1 [icon:YIELD_SCIENCE] Science, rising to +2 in the Modern Age, plus a Science bonus that grows by about +1.5 for each immigrant you attract, up to +12 per turn. This bonus fades if immigration stops.

[icon:YIELD_POPULATION] Newcomers enrich your culture. Gain +1 [icon:YIELD_CULTURE] Culture, rising to +2 in the Modern Age, plus a Culture bonus that grows by about +1.5 for each immigrant you attract, up to +12 per turn. This bonus fades if immigration stops.

[icon:YIELD_POPULATION] Newcomers swell your markets. Gain +1 [icon:YIELD_GOLD] Gold, rising to +2 in the Modern Age, plus a Gold bonus that grows by about +1.5 for each immigrant you attract, up to +12 per turn. This bonus fades if immigration stops.
Open your doors to those fleeing war and disaster. Refugees are strongly drawn to you, the most desperate first, and your humanitarian standing earns +1 [icon:YIELD_DIPLOMACY] Influence each turn.

A standing commitment to the displaced. Refugees flow strongly toward you, the most desperate first, enriching your culture and earning +2 [icon:YIELD_DIPLOMACY] Influence and +1 [icon:YIELD_CULTURE] Culture each turn.

Emigration adds living population movement to the world. Each turn, citizens leave unhappy, struggling, or war-torn settlements and resettle in happier, more prosperous ones, within your civilization and across borders to others.[n][n]Where people go is decided by a Prosperity model that weighs each city's happiness, yields, and situation, shaped by distance so migration stays regional. Receiving migrants is not free: a civilization pays an assimilation cost as newcomers integrate.[n][n]The world also keeps a memory of who moved. Every settlement tracks the civilizations its people descend from, shown on the map by the Ethnic Composition lens. Newcomers slowly take on their host's identity, diasporas return home once their homeland recovers, and the great waves are written up as history in the Migration Chronicle.[n][n]Most of the system's deeper behaviors (refugees that shun an aggressor, border policies, disasters, ethnic integration, return migration, and the occasional refugee decision) are on by default and can be switched off in Options, under Mods, Emigration.

What does and does not drive migration (most of it tunable in Options, under Mods, Emigration). A full version is on the dashboard's Guide tab.[n][n]✓ Your civilization and other major civilizations move population, whether or not you have met them.[n]✗ City-states, minor civilizations, and Independent Powers do not send or receive population.[n]✓ Unhappiness, low yields, war damage, sieges (including attacks by a city-state or Independent Power), pillaging, starvation, plague, disasters, and overcrowding all push people out.[n]✓ Prosperity, high happiness, a Pro-Immigration stance, Open Borders, and a nearby location attract them.[n]✗ Migrants are not instant (distance adds delay) and not free (a temporary assimilation cost); war alone cannot empty a city without capturing it.[n][n]Emigration does not change AI decisions or replace base-game files. Unmet civilizations are simulated but hidden until you reveal them.

Every settlement is scored for Prosperity from its per-capita yields and, above all, its net happiness. An unhappy city steadily loses people to happier, wealthier ones even in peacetime, while a small penalty for sheer size keeps thriving towns from becoming bottomless magnets.[n][n]People prefer nearby destinations, so migration spreads outward across the region rather than leaping across the map. War, siege, starvation, unrest, and disaster all sharply lower a city's Prosperity, turning it into a source of refugees.

A city only sheds war refugees when there is actual violence inside its borders, its districts taking damage, or pillaging on its land, not merely because its empire is at war. The longer a siege grinds on, the more it drives people out, but a city can never be fully emptied by war alone: short of an outright capture, a remnant always digs in.[n][n]Refugees flee away from the nearest invader. With aggressor avoidance enabled, they also prefer their own civilization first, then any neutral power, and treat the civilization that attacked them as a destination of last resort.[n][n]People who flee are not gone forever. Once their original homeland is at peace with their host and prospering again, a fraction of those living abroad set out for home over time, a slow return rather than a snap-back. And when a great wave reaches your own lands, driven by a neighbor's conquests or a plague, you are occasionally asked how to receive them: take them in, settle them on the frontier, or turn them away. These refugee decisions are rare by design and can be switched off in Options.

Absorbing migrants is not free. Each newcomer adds an assimilation load to the receiving civilization, which pays a per-turn cost (in gold and in happiness) until that load fades over the integration period. A civilization that keeps pulling people in keeps paying.[n][n]A congestion effect can further make a civilization that is busy digesting migrants a less attractive destination for still more, a brake that wealth alone does not remove. Together these keep any single civilization from snowballing into a dominant migration magnet for the world's people.[n][n]Assimilation also has a slower, cultural side, separate from the gold and happiness cost above. Over many turns, newcomers take on their host civilization's identity within each settlement's ethnic makeup, so a peaceful, settled city gradually absorbs a diaspora. War with a minority's homeland, or unrest in the city, holds that integration back, leaving a distinct community that keeps its color on the Ethnic Composition lens.

Two levers shape migration across your borders. Your own stance is a policy card, available in every age. Slot the Pro-Immigration Stance to welcome the world: more migrants arrive, and your cosmopolitan reputation earns a little Influence each turn. Or slot the Anti-Immigration Stance to close yourself off: fewer foreigners arrive and fewer of your own people leave for rivals, and a mobilized home economy adds Production in every city, though the world judges your isolation with a steady Influence drain. Choose whichever fits your strategy.[n][n]Diplomacy matters too. When you and another civilization sign an Open Borders agreement, migrants flow more freely between your two lands, in both directions.

Where the stance policies decide whether your borders are open, a second set of cards decides what you make of the people who pass through them. The Talent, Cultural, and Commercial Attraction policies turn immigration into an engine: every newcomer you draw banks a growing dividend of Science, Culture, or Gold that compounds while people keep arriving and fades once the inflow stops, a counterpart to the assimilation cost, rewarding civilizations that sustain attraction.[n][n]The Asylum policies answer a different call. Throwing your doors open to those fleeing war and catastrophe draws refugees toward you, the most desperate first, and the world remembers the gesture in Influence, with the later compact enriching your culture besides. Together they let a welcoming people benefit from openness rather than merely endure it.

Attraction need not wait for migration to come to you. From the diplomacy screen you can aim a Talent Raid, Cultural Offensive, or Commercial Offensive at a rival, a sustained campaign to bend their people toward your cities. For as long as it runs you pay Influence each turn, and in return that rival's migration tilts your way, drawing off their brightest minds, cultural figures, or merchants; each one who arrives banks the matching Science, Culture, or Gold dividend, the same as a willing immigrant.[n][n]It is a strategic competition tool, not war: no city is attacked and no border is crossed by force, yet a stronger neighbor's talent can be quietly drained to your benefit for the price of sustained diplomatic effort.

Environmental disasters (floods, volcanoes, plagues, and more) drive people out of stricken cities as climate and disaster refugees, alongside war and unhappiness. The worse the event, the harder it pushes; minor events only nudge migration quietly.[n][n]Plague is doubly dangerous: an infected city both loses population and, if migrants carry the contagion, can seed a smaller outbreak wherever its refugees settle, spreading disease along the very roads of flight.

Migration is not a sealed system. When a city is desperate, under siege, starving, or devastated by disaster, and there is nowhere safe left to flee, its trapped population does not simply wait forever. People die or scatter, and the city loses population with no destination.[n][n]This outlet is a measure of last resort: a content city with merely nowhere better to go is left untouched, and the loss is counted as deaths, kept distinct from the refugees who actually reach a new home.

A handful of leaders and civilizations feel migration differently, so their historical character shows through. These are small, bounded adjustments on top of the global rules, not large enough to trigger runaway growth, and they apply only while per-civ tuning is on (it is, by default).[n][n]Leaders whose happiness would otherwise make cities dominant migration magnets pull a little less strongly: Benjamin Franklin most of all, with Isabella, Ashoka, and Rizal also reined in. Wealthy conquerors find spoils costlier to settle, Isabella and Xerxes pay more to absorb the people they win.[n][n]Tall, specialist-minded powers are shielded from overcrowding so their dense cities aren't punished twice: Pachacuti, and the Abbasids most of all. Fast-growing peoples whose numbers outrun their prosperity get a small cushion to keep their citizens, Confucius, the Han, and the Qing, while the Khmer are spared the worst of their own non-capital unhappiness. And realms built behind strong walls, the Normans and the English, hold their population through a siege far better than the unwalled.

Emigration simulates the whole world from the first turn: every living major civilization, not only the ones you have met. This keeps migration honest, people flow according to the real state of the world, and a city you later conquer carries the true origin history of its people.[n][n]To avoid spoilers, the dashboard, the graphs, and the map lens only SHOW civilizations you have met. Unmet civilizations are still calculated in the background, but hidden from view by default, so you don't learn about rivals you haven't encountered yet. You can widen what is shown through the shared analytics-visibility setting (the same one the Demographics mod uses): your own civilization only, met civilizations, or all.[n][n]In short: everything is simulated, and what you see is up to you. Simulation scope (whole world or met-only) and on-screen visibility are independent settings.

Open the Migration dashboard from its button on the in-game dock, or from the Migration page in the Demographics mod. It is organized into tabs:[n][n]• A migration network, an animated view of civilizations sized by how much population moves through them, with dots flowing along the routes.[n]• A flow map, the same flows drawn as arrows you can follow down to individual cities.[n]• A civilizations ledger of arrivals, departures, net change, refugees, and losses.[n]• A breakdown of why people move, by cause.[n]• Your settlements, ranked by how close each is to losing people.[n]• Immigration policies, showing who has adopted a Pro- or Anti-Immigration stance.[n]• A Migration Chronicle, a written history of the world's great migrations: cities emptied by war or disaster, diasporas taking root far from home, and peoples returning once their homeland recovered.[n]• A Guide with a quick reference of what does and does not drive migration.[n][n]On the map itself, the Ethnic Composition lens (Shift+E) paints each settlement as a per-tile mosaic by the origin of its people, the dense urban core in the dominant origin's color and minorities on the rural fringe, and hovering a settled tile adds the exact mix to its tooltip.

Every settlement records where its people came from, by the civilization each person descends from, and keeps that record through conquest. The Ethnic Composition lens (Shift+E) shows it as a per-tile mosaic: the dense urban core in the dominant origin's color, minorities on the rural fringe, with the exact mix in each settled tile's tooltip.[n][n]Over time, newcomers integrate into their host civilization, unless war with their homeland or unrest in the city keeps a minority distinct. And once a homeland is at peace with its host and prospering again, return migration draws a fraction of its people back home over time, moving real population. All of this is on by default and can be switched off in Options.

The Migration Chronicle, on its own tab in the dashboard, writes the world's great migrations as short history: a city emptied by war or disaster, a diaspora taking root far from home, a people returning once their homeland recovered. The per-event detail still lives in the Notifications log; the Chronicle is the story.[n][n]Once in a while, when a real upheaval (a neighbor conquering several cities in a short span, or a plague crisis) sends a wave of refugees toward your lands, you are asked how to receive them: welcome them in, settle them on the frontier, or turn them away. These decisions are rare and lightly weighted, and can be switched off in Options. A civilization you have not met is named only as hearsay, never revealed.
Show live migration in the dashboard, or sample data to preview the visualization before real cross-civ flows build up.
How often the migration network records a snapshot for the timeline scrubber: fewer turns per snapshot gives finer playback, at a small cost in save size.
Show the Migration button on the in-game dock, opening the standalone dashboard window. The full dashboard (and the migration graphs) always also live in the Demographics screen's Migration tab.
No cross-civ migration yet, flows appear once people cross borders.

Each dot ≈ {1_People} people. A circle is one civilization, holding its cities and towns; its dots are home-grown residents (its own colour), people who moved between its cities (a lighter tint), and immigrants (their origin's colour). Recolour with "Color by", filter with the Show/Origins toggles, click a swatch or circle to isolate it, and press ▶ or scrub the timeline to replay history.
Show lines to where migrants came from
